1:
"And Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur, and sojourned in Gerar." The witnesses note Abraham's journey to and sojourn in Gerar. On "Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country," the grammatical voices note the move. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur" — Abraham left Mamre (where he had dwelt, 13:18; 18:1) and journeyed southward, into the region between Kadesh and Shur (toward the Negev and the border of Egypt); the patriarch, a pilgrim and stranger (Hebrews 11:9), moving his tents; perhaps moved by the destruction of Sodom (ch. 19), the region near it now desolate.

On "sojourned in Gerar," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the place. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham "sojourned in Gerar" — a city of the Philistines (26:1), ruled by Abimelech; the patriarch dwelling as a stranger among the Philistines; the sojourn that would bring the incident with Abimelech (vv.2-18).

On the sojourn, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the pilgrim life: Abraham's journeying and sojourning mark his life as a stranger and pilgrim, dwelling in tents, moving from place to place, possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5); the setting for the second sister-incident; the patriarch among a strange people. The verse displays Abraham's journey to and sojourn in Gerar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the move — "Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur," Abraham moving southward from Mamre (13:18; 18:1, Ellicott, Gill); "sojourned in Gerar," the patriarch dwelling among the Philistines (26:1, Calvin, Gill); the pilgrim life of Abraham (Hebrews 11:9; Acts 7:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Abraham. The verse marks the journey: southward. The verse displays the place: Gerar. The verse displays the sojourn: among the Philistines. The verse displays the pilgrim: Abraham. The journey displays Abraham moving as a stranger to Gerar. The verse displays the Abraham: journeying. The verse displays the Gerar: sojourned in. The verse displays the pilgrim: life. Abraham's journey to and sojourn in Gerar display the pilgrim patriarch among a strange people: Abraham journeyed south and sojourned in Gerar — the stranger and pilgrim moving his tents, dwelling among the Philistines, the setting for the incident with Abimelech. The verse displays the sojourning Abraham, who calls Sarah his sister (v.2).
2:
"And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah." The witnesses note Abraham's repeated deception over Sarah — the crux of the relapse. On "Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister," interpreters note the repeated fault. The reformed Gill and witnesses note that "Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister" — Abraham again gave out that Sarah was his sister (as in Egypt, 12:13); the same infirmity, the same evil into which he had fallen before; the sad relapse of the patriarch into the same sin of unbelief and the fear of man (v.11), not learning from the first deliverance (12:17-20); the famed father of faith falling twice into the same fault.

On "She is my sister," the grammatical-historical Keil and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the half-truth. Keil and Clarke note that the saying was a half-truth — Sarah was indeed Abraham's half-sister (the daughter of his father, not his mother, v.12); not a direct lie, but an equivocation meant to conceal that she was his wife, and so to deceive; the suppression of the whole truth; the fault of unbelief, trusting his own contrivance rather than God's protection; the same equivocation as before (12:13).

On "Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the consequence: "Abimelech... sent, and took Sarah" — the king of Gerar, supposing her unmarried, took Sarah (into his house, to be his wife or concubine); the deception again endangered Sarah (now about ninety, yet still fair, or honored as a princess) and the promise (the seed, soon to be born, 17:21; 18:10); the stratagem of unbelief bringing peril, requiring God's intervention (vv.3-7). The verse displays Abraham's repeated deception over Sarah.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham again called Sarah his sister, and Abimelech took her (Gill, Keil, Clarke, Calvin). The act bears a crux: a repeated fault of unbelief and the fear of man — the same sin as in Egypt (12:13), the relapse of the patriarch, not learning from the first (the dominant judgment) vs an equivocation literally true (Sarah his half-sister, v.12), the half-truth meant to deceive yet not a direct lie; in either case a fault of weak faith that endangered Sarah and the promise. The verse displays the repeated-deception crux. The verse marks the saying: she is my sister. The verse displays the debate: a relapse into the same sin, or an equivocation. The verse displays the Abimelech: took Sarah. The verse displays the promise: endangered. The deception displays Abraham's repeated fault of unbelief. The verse displays the Abraham: dissembling. The verse displays the Sarah: taken. The verse displays the fault: repeated. Abraham's repeated deception over Sarah displays his relapse and a crux: Abraham said, She is my sister, and Abimelech took Sarah — read as the same fault of unbelief and the fear of man (12:13), the patriarch's sad relapse, or as an equivocation literally true (his half-sister, v.12); the weak faith that again endangered Sarah and the promise. The verse displays the taken Sarah, warned of by God (v.3).
3:
"But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a man's wife." The witnesses note God's warning to Abimelech in a dream. On "God came to Abimelech in a dream by night," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Ellicott and Gill note that "God came to Abimelech in a dream by night" — God revealed himself to the heathen king Abimelech in a dream; the divine intervention to protect Sarah and the promise; God dealing even with a pagan king by revelation; the means by which God warned and restrained Abimelech.

On "Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the warning. Henry and Gill note that God warned Abimelech, "thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken" — the threat of death for taking another man's wife; the solemn warning of judgment; God's protection of Sarah's honor by threatening the king; the danger Abimelech was in (though innocent in intention, v.5).

On "for she is a man's wife," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the cause: "for she is a man's wife" — the ground of the warning was that Sarah was a married woman (Abraham's wife), whatever Abimelech had been told; God's regard to the marriage bond and to Sarah; the deception (v.2) exposed by God; the LORD intervening to prevent the wrong and protect the promise. The verse displays God's warning to Abimelech in a dream.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the revelation — "God came to Abimelech in a dream by night," God revealing himself to the heathen king (Ellicott, Gill); "thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken," the warning of death (Henry, Gill); "for she is a man's wife," the cause, Sarah married (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warned Abimelech. The verse marks the dream: God came. The verse displays the warning: a dead man. The verse displays the woman: a man's wife. The verse displays the protection: of Sarah. The revelation displays God warning the king to protect Sarah. The verse displays the God: warning. The verse displays the Abimelech: threatened. The verse displays the wife: revealed. God's warning to Abimelech in a dream displays the divine protection of Sarah: God came to Abimelech, saying, Thou art but a dead man, for the woman thou hast taken is a man's wife — God intervening to warn the king, protecting Sarah's honor and the promise, exposing the deception. The verse displays the warned Abimelech, who pleads his innocence (vv.4-5).
4:
"But Abimelech had not come near her: and he said, Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?" The witnesses note Abimelech's not having touched Sarah and his plea of innocence. On "Abimelech had not come near her," the grammatical voices note the providence. Gill and JFB note that "Abimelech had not come near her" — Abimelech had not yet touched Sarah (God having restrained him, v.6); the providence of God preserving Sarah's honor; the king kept from the sin; the protection of the promise (the seed soon to be born, 17:21).

On "Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the plea. Calvin and Gill note that Abimelech pleaded, "Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?" — protesting that he (and his nation) had acted innocently, not knowing Sarah was married; the appeal to God's justice, that he would not slay the innocent; the king's conscience clear of intentional wrong; the plea of one who had been deceived.

On "a righteous nation," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the appeal: "wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?" — Abimelech appealed that he and his people were guiltless in this matter (having been deceived, v.5); the protest of integrity; the appeal to the Judge of all the earth (cf. 18:25) not to destroy the innocent; the heathen king's sense of justice. The verse displays Abimelech's not having touched Sarah and his plea.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the providence — "Abimelech had not come near her," God restraining him, Sarah preserved (v.6, Gill, JFB); "Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?" Abimelech's plea of innocence (Calvin, Gill); the appeal to God's justice not to slay the innocent (18:25, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading Abimelech. The verse marks the touching: not come near. The verse displays the plea: a righteous nation. The verse displays the innocence: protested. The verse displays the justice: appealed to. The plea displays Abimelech protesting his innocence. The verse displays the Abimelech: pleading. The verse displays the Sarah: untouched. The verse displays the innocence: claimed. Abimelech's not having touched Sarah and his plea of innocence display the providence and the king's conscience: Abimelech had not come near her, and he pleaded, Lord, wilt thou slay a righteous nation? — God having preserved Sarah, the king protesting his innocence, appealing to God's justice not to destroy the guiltless. The verse displays the pleading Abimelech, recounting the deception (v.5).
5:
"Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself said, He is my brother: in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this." The witnesses note Abimelech's protest of his integrity and innocence. On "Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she... said, He is my brother," the grammatical voices note the deception. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself said, He is my brother" — Abimelech recounted that both Abraham and Sarah had told him they were brother and sister; the deception had come from both (Sarah complying with Abraham's plan, 12:13; 20:13); the king deceived by their words; his innocence in acting on what he was told.

On "in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the protest. Calvin and Gill note that Abimelech protested "in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this" — his sincerity of heart (no evil intent, not knowing Sarah was married) and his innocence of hand (not having touched her, v.4); the king's clear conscience in the matter; the protest of one who had acted in good faith; the integrity that God himself acknowledged (v.6).

On "integrity... innocency," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the good faith: Abimelech's "integrity of heart and innocency of hands" mark his good faith — he had not intended adultery, having been deceived; the heathen king's conscience; God acknowledged his integrity (v.6), yet had restrained him from the actual sin; the distinction between intentional and unintentional wrong. The verse displays Abimelech's protest of his integrity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deception — "Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she... said, He is my brother," both having deceived the king (12:13; 20:13, Keil, Gill); "in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands," Abimelech's protest of good faith (Calvin, Gill); God acknowledging his integrity, yet restraining him (v.6, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the protesting Abimelech. The verse marks the deception: both said it. The verse displays the integrity: of heart. The verse displays the innocency: of hands. The verse displays the good faith: of the king. The protest displays Abimelech's clear conscience in the matter. The verse displays the Abimelech: protesting. The verse displays the integrity: claimed. The verse displays the innocence: pleaded. Abimelech's protest of his integrity displays his good faith: both said they were brother and sister; in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this — the king deceived by their words, protesting his sincerity and clear conscience, which God acknowledged (v.6). The verse displays the protesting Abimelech, answered by God (v.6).
6:
"And God said unto him in a dream, Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me: therefore suffered I thee not to touch her." The witnesses note God restraining Abimelech from sin — the crux of restraining grace. On "I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart," the grammatical voices note the acknowledgment. Gill and Calvin note that "Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart" — God acknowledged Abimelech's sincerity (v.5); the king had acted without evil intent, being deceived; God, who knows the heart, confirmed his good faith; the divine recognition of the king's integrity in the matter.

On "for I also withheld thee from sinning against me," interpreters expound the restraining grace. The reformed Calvin and Gill note the great word — "I also withheld thee from sinning against me" — God himself had restrained Abimelech from committing the sin (touching Sarah); the doctrine of restraining (preventing) grace: God keeps men, even the heathen and the wicked, from sins they would otherwise commit; whether by inward restraint (the Spirit bridling the heart, holding it back), or by his providence (taking away the opportunity, here perhaps by an affliction, v.17, or by some hindrance); God's sovereign hand in preventing sin; the LORD restraining the king to protect Sarah and the promise. The sin would have been "against me (God)" — for all sin, even against man, is ultimately against God (Psalm 51:4).

On "therefore suffered I thee not to touch her," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the means: "therefore suffered I thee not to touch her" — God did not permit Abimelech to touch Sarah; the actual preservation of her honor; the restraining grace effecting the protection; the providence that kept the king from the sin; God's care for Sarah and the covenant-seed. The verse displays God restraining Abimelech from sin.

The witnesses agree the substance: God acknowledged Abimelech's integrity and declared that he himself had withheld him from sinning, not suffering him to touch Sarah (Gill, Calvin, Wesley). The verse founds the doctrine of restraining grace: God restrains men, even the heathen, from sins they would commit — by inward restraint of the Spirit, or by his providence removing the opportunity; all sin being ultimately against God; the LORD's sovereign hand preventing the wrong to protect Sarah and the promise. The verse displays the restraining-grace crux. The verse marks the integrity: acknowledged. The verse displays the restraint: I withheld thee. The verse displays the sin: against me. The verse displays the protection: not to touch her. The word displays God's sovereign restraining of sin. The verse displays the God: withholding. The verse displays the Abimelech: restrained. The verse displays the grace: preventing. God restraining Abimelech from sin displays the doctrine of restraining grace: I withheld thee from sinning against me, and suffered thee not to touch her — God's sovereign hand keeping the king, even a heathen, from the sin (by inward restraint or by providence), all sin being against God, to protect Sarah and the promise. The verse displays the restrained Abimelech, bidden to restore Sarah (v.7).
7:
"Now therefore restore the man his wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live: and if thou restore her not, know thou that thou shalt surely die, thou, and all that are thine." The witnesses note the command to restore Sarah, and Abraham named a prophet who shall pray — the crux of "prophet." On "restore the man his wife," the grammatical voices note the command. Gill and Ellicott note that "Now therefore restore the man his wife" — God commanded Abimelech to give Sarah back to Abraham; the requirement to undo the wrong (though unintentional); the restoring of Sarah to her husband; the way of safety for the king.

On "for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee," interpreters expound the title. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note that Abraham is called "a prophet" (nabi) — the first occurrence of the word "prophet" in Scripture; Clarke notes that "prophet" here does not mean primarily a foreteller of the future, but (the Hebrew nabi) one who intercedes, who speaks to God for men (and from God to men); the prophet as the intercessor, the man near to God whose prayers prevail; Abraham, the friend of God, named a prophet whose intercession would heal (v.17); the office of the prophet as intercessor and revealer. Some take "prophet" in the fuller sense (one to whom God reveals himself, vv.3-7, and who speaks for God); the title marks Abraham's special relation to God.

On "he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live... if thou restore her not... thou shalt surely die," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the alternative: Abraham's prayer would obtain Abimelech's life and healing (v.17); but if the king restored not Sarah, he and all his would die; the offer of life through the prophet's intercession, and the threat of death for disobedience; the power of the prophet's prayer; the way of life set before the king. The verse displays the command to restore Sarah and the prophet's intercession.

The witnesses agree the substance: God commanded Abimelech to restore Sarah, for Abraham was a prophet whose prayer would save him (Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The title "prophet" (nabi) bears its first occurrence as a crux: a prophet in the sense of the intercessor — one who speaks to God for men, whose prayer prevails (Clarke) vs a prophet in the fuller sense — one to whom God reveals himself and who speaks for God (the broader reading); in either, Abraham the man near to God whose intercession heals (v.17). The verse displays the prophet crux. The verse marks the command: restore Sarah. The verse displays the debate: prophet as intercessor, or as revealer. The verse displays the prayer: heals. The verse displays the death: threatened. The command displays God offering life through the prophet's prayer. The verse displays the Abraham: a prophet. The verse displays the intercession: prevailing. The verse displays the life: offered. The command to restore Sarah and the prophet's intercession display the way of life and a crux: restore his wife, for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee — Abraham, the first named "prophet" (nabi), read as the intercessor whose prayer prevails (Clarke) or as the revealer who speaks for God; his prayer would heal Abimelech (v.17), the alternative being death. The verse displays the warned Abimelech, who rises early (v.8).
8:
"Therefore Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ears: and the men were sore afraid." The witnesses note Abimelech's prompt response and his servants' fear. On "Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants," the grammatical voices note the haste. Gill and Henry note that "Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ears" — the king, alarmed by the dream (vv.3-7), rose early and told his servants all that God had said; the prompt, serious response to the divine warning; the king sharing the matter with his court.

On "rose early in the morning," the puritan Henry notes the diligence. Henry and Gill note that Abimelech rose early — the urgency and seriousness of his response to God's warning; the king's care to obey and to set things right; the diligence prompted by the fear of God's threat.

On "the men were sore afraid," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the fear: "the men were sore afraid" — the servants, hearing of God's warning and the danger (death threatened to the king and all his, v.7), were greatly afraid; the fear of God's judgment falling on the household; the dread that the king's act had endangered them all; the awe of the divine warning. The verse displays Abimelech's prompt response and his servants' fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the haste — "Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things," the prompt response to the warning (Gill, Henry); "rose early in the morning," the diligence and seriousness (Henry, Gill); "the men were sore afraid," the servants' fear of the judgment (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the alarmed court. The verse marks the rising: early. The verse displays the servants: told. The verse displays the fear: sore. The verse displays the warning: heeded. The response displays Abimelech acting promptly on the warning. The verse displays the king: rising. The verse displays the court: afraid. The verse displays the warning: taken. Abimelech's prompt response and his servants' fear display the seriousness of the warning: Abimelech rose early, told his servants, and the men were sore afraid — the king's prompt, serious response to the divine warning, the court's dread of the judgment that the act had threatened. The verse displays the alarmed Abimelech, who rebukes Abraham (vv.9-10).
9:
"Then Abimelech called Abraham, and said unto him, What hast thou done unto us? and what have I offended thee, that thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done." The witnesses note Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham. On "What hast thou done unto us?" the grammatical voices note the reproof. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abimelech called Abraham, and said... What hast thou done unto us?" — the king reproved Abraham for the deception that had endangered him and his kingdom; the heathen king rebuking the patriarch; the just reproof for the fault (as Pharaoh, 12:18); the humbling of Abraham, reproved by a pagan.

On "thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the danger. Henry and Calvin note that Abraham's deception "brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin" — the king and his people had been brought near to a great sin (adultery, unwittingly) and to the judgment it would have incurred; the deception had endangered the whole kingdom; the gravity of the consequence of Abraham's fault.

On "thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the reproach: "thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done" — Abimelech reproached Abraham for acting wrongly, doing what ought not to be done; the just complaint against the deception; the patriarch, who should have been a witness of truth, reproved by a heathen for his fault; the shame of Abraham's relapse (cf. 12:18). The verse displays Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reproof — "What hast thou done unto us?" the king reproving Abraham (Keil, Gill); "thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin," the danger to the whole kingdom (Henry, Calvin); "thou hast done deeds... that ought not to be done," the just reproach (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebuked Abraham. The verse marks the reproof: what hast thou done. The verse displays the sin: brought on the kingdom. The verse displays the deeds: that ought not. The verse displays the shame: of Abraham. The rebuke displays the heathen king reproving the patriarch. The verse displays the Abimelech: reproving. The verse displays the Abraham: humbled. The verse displays the fault: exposed. Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham displays the humbling of the believer for his fault: what hast thou done? thou hast brought on me and my kingdom a great sin — the heathen king justly reproving the patriarch for the deception that endangered him, the shame of Abraham's relapse (12:18). The verse displays the rebuked Abraham, questioned of his motive (v.10).
10:
"And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?" The very brief verse notes Abimelech asking Abraham's reason for the deception. The grammatical voices note the question. Gill and Ellicott note that "Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?" — the king asked what Abraham had seen or thought (what reason or fear) that led him to the deception; the inquiry into the motive; the king pressing Abraham to explain his fault; the demand for an account of his conduct.

On "What sawest thou," the witnesses note the inquiry. Gill notes that the king asked what Abraham "sawest" (perceived, feared, or had in view) that prompted the deception; the inquiry into Abraham's reasoning; the king seeking to understand the patriarch's motive.

On "that thou hast done this thing," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the demand: "that thou hast done this thing" — Abimelech demanded an explanation of Abraham's act; the call for the patriarch to give account; the question that drew out Abraham's reasoning (vv.11-13) — his fear (v.11), the half-truth (v.12), and his practice (v.13); the king's just inquiry. The verse displays Abimelech asking Abraham's reason for the deception.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the question — "What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?" the king asking Abraham's reason (Gill, Ellicott); the inquiry into the motive (Gill); the demand for an account, drawing out Abraham's reasoning (vv.11-13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inquiring Abimelech. The verse marks the question: what sawest thou. The verse displays the reason: asked. The verse displays the deed: this thing. The verse displays the account: demanded. The question displays Abimelech seeking Abraham's motive. The verse displays the Abimelech: asking. The verse displays the Abraham: questioned. The verse displays the reason: sought. Abimelech asking Abraham's reason for the deception displays the demand for an account: What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing? — the king inquiring into the patriarch's motive, drawing out his reasoning (his fear, the half-truth, his practice, vv.11-13). The verse displays the questioned Abraham, who explains (vv.11-13).
11:
"And Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife's sake." The witnesses note Abraham's reason — his fear that there was no fear of God in Gerar. On "Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place," the grammatical voices note the supposition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place" — Abraham explained his deception: he had supposed that there was no fear of God in Gerar (among the heathen Philistines), and so no restraint on wickedness; the patriarch's reasoning that, in a godless place, his life would be in danger for his wife's sake.

On "the fear of God is not in this place," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the misjudgment. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham's supposition was, in part, a misjudgment — he had presumed the worst of the place, yet there was found more fear of God in Abimelech (v.5-6) than Abraham had expected; the patriarch's fear, born of his prejudgment, led him into the fault; the warning against presuming the worst and acting on fear rather than faith; (yet the fear of God was indeed lacking enough that such things were a real danger).

On "they will slay me for my wife's sake," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the fear: "they will slay me for my wife's sake" — Abraham feared that the men of Gerar, lacking the fear of God, would kill him to take Sarah (as he had feared in Egypt, 12:12); the fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) driving the deception; the weakness of faith, looking to the danger rather than trusting God's protection; the same fear that had led to the first fault. The verse displays Abraham's reason — his fear of a godless place.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the supposition — "I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place," Abraham presuming no fear of God in Gerar (Keil, Gill); the misjudgment, more fear of God being found than expected (vv.5-6, Calvin, Gill); "they will slay me for my wife's sake," the fear of man driving the deception (12:12; Proverbs 29:25, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fearful Abraham. The verse marks the supposition: no fear of God. The verse displays the danger: slain for Sarah. The verse displays the misjudgment: of the place. The verse displays the fear: of man. The reason displays Abraham's fear-driven deception. The verse displays the Abraham: fearing. The verse displays the place: misjudged. The verse displays the fault: explained. Abraham's reason — his fear that there was no fear of God in Gerar — displays the fault born of fear: I thought the fear of God is not in this place, and they will slay me for my wife's sake — the patriarch's misjudgment and the fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) driving the deception, the weakness of faith looking to the danger. The verse displays the explaining Abraham, pleading the half-truth (v.12).
12:
"And yet indeed she is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife." The witnesses note Abraham's defense — Sarah his half-sister. On "she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother," the grammatical voices note the relation. Gill and Clarke note that "she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother" — Abraham defended his saying (v.2) as a half-truth: Sarah was indeed his half-sister (the daughter of his father Terah by another mother, or some take her his niece, called "sister" in the loose usage of kindred, cf. 11:29; the relation variously reckoned); the saying "she is my sister" was thus literally true, in a sense; the equivocation rested on a real kinship.

On "she is my sister," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the equivocation. Clarke and Gill note that Abraham pleaded the literal truth of his words — Sarah was his sister (half-sister or near kin); the saying was not a direct lie but a suppression of the fuller truth (that she was also his wife); the equivocation, while resting on a fact, was meant to deceive; the fault lay in the concealment and the intent, not in a bare falsehood.

On "she became my wife," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the concealed truth: "she became my wife" — the truth Abraham concealed was that Sarah, his sister, had become his wife; the marriage (of near kin, before the later law forbidding such, Leviticus 18:9) was the fact suppressed; the half-truth concealed the whole; the fault of the deception, however the kinship be reckoned. The verse displays Abraham's defense that Sarah was his half-sister.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the relation — "she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother," Sarah Abraham's half-sister (or near kin, 11:29, Gill, Clarke); "she is my sister," the equivocation resting on a real kinship (Clarke, Gill); "she became my wife," the truth concealed (Leviticus 18:9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defending Abraham. The verse marks the relation: half-sister. The verse displays the saying: literally true. The verse displays the wife: concealed. The verse displays the equivocation: defended. The defense displays Abraham pleading the half-truth. The verse displays the Sarah: his sister. The verse displays the truth: concealed. The verse displays the fault: in the intent. Abraham's defense that Sarah was his half-sister displays the half-truth: she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not my mother, and she became my wife — the saying literally true (resting on a real kinship), yet the whole truth (that she was his wife) concealed, the fault in the deceiving intent. The verse displays the defending Abraham, owning his practice (v.13).
13:
"And it came to pass, when God caused me to wander from my father's house, that I said unto her, This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me; at every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother." The witnesses note Abraham's confession of his long-standing practice. On "when God caused me to wander from my father's house," the grammatical voices note the pilgrimage. Gill and Clarke note that "when God caused me to wander from my father's house" — Abraham referred to his call and pilgrimage (12:1; the wandering, sojourning life of the patriarch, a stranger in the land); from the beginning of his wandering, he had made this arrangement with Sarah; the long-standing practice begun at his call.

On "This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the agreement. Calvin and Gill note that Abraham had said to Sarah, "This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me" — he had asked her, as a kindness, to support the arrangement; the agreement between them, made at the outset of their wandering; the practice settled long before.

On "at every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the standing arrangement: "at every place... say of me, He is my brother" — Abraham's standing policy, to call himself Sarah's brother wherever they went; the deception not a single act but a settled practice; the patriarch's habitual reliance on this stratagem (born of fear); the fault, long-continued, exposed; the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful, leaning on his own contrivance through the years. The verse displays Abraham's confession of his long-standing practice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the pilgrimage — "when God caused me to wander from my father's house," Abraham's call and wandering life (12:1, Gill, Clarke); "This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me," the agreement with Sarah (Calvin, Gill); "at every place... say of me, He is my brother," the standing, habitual practice (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confessing Abraham. The verse marks the wandering: from his father's house. The verse displays the kindness: asked of Sarah. The verse displays the practice: standing. The verse displays the fault: long-continued. The confession displays Abraham's habitual stratagem. The verse displays the Abraham: confessing. The verse displays the practice: settled. The verse displays the fault: exposed. Abraham's confession of his long-standing practice displays the settled stratagem: from my wandering I asked Sarah, at every place say of me, He is my brother — the deception a long-continued policy, born of fear, the weakness of faith in the patriarch leaning on his own contrivance through the years. The verse displays the confessing Abraham, with Sarah restored and gifts given (vv.14-16).
14:
"And Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife." The witnesses note Abimelech restoring Sarah and giving gifts to Abraham. On "Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham," the grammatical voices note the gifts. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham" — the king gave Abraham generous gifts (flocks, herds, servants); the conciliation of the patriarch (and perhaps amends for the wrong of taking Sarah); the honoring of Abraham, whom God had warned the king to regard (v.7); the king's liberality.

On "restored him Sarah his wife," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the restoration. Henry and Calvin note that Abimelech "restored him Sarah his wife" — the king returned Sarah, untouched (v.6), to Abraham; the deliverance of Sarah, by God's protecting providence; the promise (the seed through Sarah) preserved; the restoring of the wife to her husband, as God had commanded (v.7).

On the gifts and restoration, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: Abimelech's restoring Sarah and enriching Abraham show God's overruling providence — Sarah preserved and Abraham honored, despite the patriarch's fault; the deliverance resting on God's faithfulness, not Abraham's wit; the LORD bringing good even out of the fault, as in Egypt (12:16, 20); the protection of the covenant-promise. The verse displays Abimelech restoring Sarah and giving gifts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gifts — "Abimelech took sheep, and oxen... and gave them unto Abraham," the king's generous gifts (Ellicott, Gill); "restored him Sarah his wife," Sarah returned untouched (v.6, Henry, Calvin); God's overruling providence, Sarah preserved and Abraham honored (12:16, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the restored Sarah. The verse marks the gifts: flocks and servants. The verse displays the Sarah: restored. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The verse displays the promise: preserved. The restoration displays God preserving Sarah and honoring Abraham. The verse displays the Abimelech: giving. The verse displays the Sarah: returned. The verse displays the providence: faithful. Abimelech restoring Sarah and giving gifts to Abraham display God's overruling providence: the king gave Abraham flocks, herds, and servants, and restored Sarah his wife — Sarah preserved and the patriarch honored, despite his fault, the deliverance resting on God's faithfulness, the promise protected. The verse displays the restored Sarah, with the land offered (v.15).
15:
"And Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee." The very brief verse notes Abimelech inviting Abraham to dwell in his land. The grammatical voices note the offer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee" — the king offered Abraham freedom to dwell anywhere in his land; the generous, conciliatory invitation; the honoring of the patriarch (whom God had warned the king to regard, v.7); the welcome to settle in Gerar.

On "my land is before thee," the witnesses note the liberality. Gill notes that Abimelech freely offered his land to Abraham — a mark of the king's good will and his fear of God (after the warning); the contrast with Pharaoh, who sent Abraham away (12:20); the generous dealing of Abimelech.

On "dwell where it pleaseth thee," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the welcome: "dwell where it pleaseth thee" — the king gave Abraham leave to settle where he chose; the welcome to the patriarch; the providence of God giving Abraham favor in the land; the sojourner welcomed, the covenant-man honored among the Philistines (cf. 21:22-34, the later covenant with Abimelech). The verse displays Abimelech inviting Abraham to dwell in his land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee," the king inviting Abraham to dwell freely (Keil, Gill); "my land is before thee," the liberality and good will of Abimelech (Gill); "dwell where it pleaseth thee," the welcome to settle (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inviting Abimelech. The verse marks the land: before Abraham. The verse displays the offer: dwell freely. The verse displays the liberality: of the king. The verse displays the welcome: to settle. The offer displays Abimelech welcoming Abraham to his land. The verse displays the Abimelech: inviting. The verse displays the Abraham: welcomed. The verse displays the favor: given. Abimelech inviting Abraham to dwell in his land displays the king's good will: my land is before thee, dwell where it pleaseth thee — the generous, conciliatory welcome of the patriarch, the providence of God giving Abraham favor among the Philistines. The verse displays the welcomed Abraham, with the gift to Sarah (v.16).
16:
"And unto Sarah he said, Behold, I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver: behold, he is to thee a covering of the eyes, unto all that are with thee, and with all other: thus she was reproved." The witnesses note the gift to Sarah and the difficult "covering of the eyes" — the crux of the phrase. On "I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver," the grammatical voices note the gift. Gill and Clarke note that "I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver" — Abimelech gave Abraham (ironically called Sarah's "brother," as she had said) a thousand pieces (shekels) of silver; a large sum; the gift to Abraham (and so to Sarah) as amends and vindication; the honoring of Sarah and her husband.

On "he is to thee a covering of the eyes," interpreters expound the difficult phrase. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the variant readings: "he (the gift, or Abraham) is to thee a covering of the eyes" — variously understood: (a) the thousand pieces of silver are a vindication, a "covering of the eyes," a public token of Sarah's innocence — to cover (stop) the eyes and mouths of any who might reproach her, that none should look on her as one wronged or guilty; the open clearing of her honor; (b) a veil to conceal her beauty — that she should go veiled, as a married woman, that her beauty not again expose her; (c) the husband (Abraham) is to her a "covering of the eyes" — her protector and guardian. The phrase is genuinely difficult; the dominant sense takes the gift as a public vindication of Sarah's honor.

On "thus she was reproved," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the close: "thus she was reproved" (or "she was righted/vindicated") — the Hebrew is variously rendered, either that Sarah was reproved (gently rebuked for her part in the deception, v.5), or that she was vindicated/righted (her honor cleared by the public gift); the closing word on the matter; whether a reproof of Sarah or her vindication, the affair was openly set right. The verse displays the gift to Sarah and the covering of the eyes.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abimelech gave a thousand pieces of silver as a public vindication of Sarah's honor (Gill, Clarke). The phrase "a covering of the eyes" bears a crux of the difficult Hebrew: a vindication/covering of the eyes — a public token of Sarah's innocence, to stop the reproach of others (the dominant reading) vs a veil to conceal her beauty vs the husband as her protector; and "thus she was reproved" read either as a gentle reproof of Sarah or as her vindication. The verse displays the covering-of-the-eyes crux. The verse marks the gift: a thousand pieces. The verse displays the debate: vindication, veil, or protector. The verse displays the honor: of Sarah. The verse displays the close: reproved or righted. The gift displays the public clearing of Sarah's honor. The verse displays the silver: given. The verse displays the eyes: covered. The verse displays the honor: vindicated. The gift to Sarah and the covering of the eyes display the vindication and a crux: I have given a thousand pieces of silver; he is to thee a covering of the eyes — read as a public vindication of Sarah's honor (the dominant reading), a veil to conceal her beauty, or the husband as her protector; "thus she was reproved" read as a gentle reproof or as her being righted. The verse displays the vindicated Sarah, healed by Abraham's prayer (vv.17-18).
17:
"So Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bare children." The witnesses note Abraham's prayer healing Abimelech's house. On "Abraham prayed unto God," the grammatical voices note the intercession. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham prayed unto God" — the patriarch, as the prophet (v.7), interceded for Abimelech; his prayer fulfilling God's word (v.7, "he shall pray for thee"); the intercession of the friend of God; the prophet's prayer for the king who had wronged him (the returning of good for the rebuke).

On "God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the healing. Calvin and Gill note that "God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants" — God, in answer to Abraham's prayer, healed Abimelech and the women of his house of the affliction (the closing of the wombs, v.18; perhaps also a disease on the king); the power of the prophet's intercession; God's mercy restoring the household.

On "they bare children," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the restoration: "they bare children" — the women, healed, again bore children (the wombs, closed by God, v.18, now opened); the removal of the judgment; the fruit of Abraham's prayer; the restoration of fruitfulness to the house; the power of the intercession of the righteous (James 5:16). The verse displays Abraham's prayer healing Abimelech's house.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the intercession — "Abraham prayed unto God," the prophet's prayer for Abimelech (v.7, Ellicott, Gill); "God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants," the healing in answer to prayer (Calvin, Gill); "they bare children," the restoration of fruitfulness (James 5:16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the praying Abraham. The verse marks the prayer: Abraham's. The verse displays the healing: of the house. The verse displays the children: borne. The verse displays the intercession: prevailing. The prayer displays Abraham's intercession healing the king's house. The verse displays the Abraham: praying. The verse displays the God: healing. The verse displays the house: restored. Abraham's prayer healing Abimelech's house displays the power of the prophet's intercession: Abraham prayed, and God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his maidservants, and they bare children — the prophet's prayer (v.7) prevailing for the king who had wronged him, the restoration of the house, the power of the righteous man's prayer (James 5:16). The verse displays the healed house, whose wombs God had closed (v.18).
18:
"For the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah Abraham's wife." The witnesses note that the LORD had closed the wombs of Abimelech's house for Sarah's sake. On "the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech," the grammatical voices note the affliction. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech" — God had visited the household with barrenness (closing the wombs of the women, and perhaps a disease on the king, vv.6, 17); the judgment (a restraint, v.6) that both prevented the sin and warned the king; the affliction that signaled God's displeasure and protected Sarah.

On "fast closed up all the wombs," the reformed and witnesses note the restraint. Gill notes that the closing of the wombs (and the affliction) was the means by which God restrained Abimelech from touching Sarah (v.6) and warned him; the providence that protected Sarah's honor; the affliction lifted by Abraham's prayer (v.17).

On "because of Sarah Abraham's wife," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the cause: "because of Sarah Abraham's wife" — the affliction came because of the taking of Sarah, Abraham's wife; God's regard to Sarah and the marriage bond; the LORD's protection of the matriarch (and the promise of the seed through her, 17:19; 18:10) by afflicting the house that had taken her; the judgment removed when Sarah was restored and Abraham prayed. The verse displays that the LORD had closed the wombs for Sarah's sake.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the affliction — "the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech," God visiting the house with barrenness (vv.6, 17, Keil, Gill); the closing the means of restraint and warning (v.6, Gill); "because of Sarah Abraham's wife," the affliction for Sarah's sake, God protecting the matriarch (17:19, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the afflicted house. The verse marks the wombs: closed. The verse displays the house: of Abimelech. The verse displays the cause: Sarah. The verse displays the protection: of the matriarch. The affliction displays God protecting Sarah by closing the wombs. The verse displays the LORD: closing. The verse displays the house: afflicted. The verse displays the Sarah: protected. That the LORD had closed the wombs of Abimelech's house for Sarah's sake displays God's protection of the matriarch: the LORD had fast closed all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah — the affliction that restrained the king and protected Sarah and the promise, removed by Abraham's prayer. The verse closes the chapter, before the birth of Isaac (ch.21).
